abstract: This paper appears to discuss empirical and practical experiences with using large language models (LLMs) for text classification and annotation in communication studies. The work likely synthesizes insights from multiple authors and research groups about both the transformative potential and emerging challenges of applying LLMs to communication research tasks. Without an abstract or full text available, specific details cannot be summarized.
key_claims: LLMs represent transformative tools for text classification and annotation in communication studies
Their adoption also brings emerging methodological and practical challenges that require empirical scrutiny
methods: Approach not specified in available materials; the title indicates a synthesis of empirical and practical experiences, likely drawing on multiple case studies or applications across the authoring teams
findings: Specific findings are not available without access to the paper's text or abstract
contributions: Likely contributes a collective assessment of LLM use for text classification and annotation tasks within communication studies
Likely highlights practical lessons and challenges relevant to researchers adopting LLMs in the field
framing: The paper situates itself within communication studies methodology, specifically the ongoing conversation about computational and automated text analysis methods, and how generative AI and large language models reshape established practices of content coding and annotation.
